@Regulatory.category: Data Used for Population PK Analysis
@Regulatory.rule_description: The population PK analysis should include data from the target patient population, and may also include data from other populations such as healthy volunteers. Key demographic, pathophysiologic, and concomitant medication information should be collected as potential covariates.
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@Regulatory.category: Data Used for Population PK Analysis
@Regulatory.rule_description: PK sampling schedules should be designed to adequately characterize the concentration-time profile, including absorption, distribution, and elimination phases. Both rich and sparse sampling designs can be informative.
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@Regulatory.rule_description: Before model development, perform a preliminary examination of the data, including graphical exploration of concentration-time data, and relationships between PK parameters and covariates.
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022
@Regulatory.source_section: V.A. Preliminary Examination of the Data

@Regulatory.rule_description: Model development is an iterative process that includes developing the structural model, the statistical model for inter-individual and residual variability, and identifying significant covariates.
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@Regulatory.rule_description: Model validation is essential to ensure the model is adequate for its intended purpose. This should include goodness-of-fit (GOF) plots, and may include techniques like bootstrap analysis and visual predictive checks (VPC).
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@Regulatory.rule_description: The population PK study report should be comprehensive and include an executive summary, introduction, materials and methods (including data, software, and models), results, discussion, and conclusions. All electronic files (datasets, model code, report) should be submitted.
@Regulatory.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022
@Regulatory.source_section: VII.A. Format and Content of the Population PK Study Report

@BioPhys.rule_description: The nonspecific clearance of therapeutic mAbs is target-independent and mediated by pinocytosis. The typical clearance for marketed mAbs ranges from 90 to 560 mL/day, with half-lives of 11-30 days, similar to endogenous IgG.
@BioPhys.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@BioPhys.rule_description: The long half-life of IgG antibodies is due to the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) recycling mechanism. FcRn binds to the IgG Fc region in a pH-dependent manner, rescuing it from lysosomal degradation and returning it to circulation.
@BioPhys.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@BioPhys.rule_description: TMDD occurs when the drug-target interaction significantly contributes to the mAb's clearance. It is characterized by nonlinear, dose-dependent clearance at non-saturating doses.
@BioPhys.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@BioPhys.rule_description: Different IgG isotypes (IgG1, IgG2, IgG4) have different effector functions (ADCC, CDC) but generally share similar long half-lives of around 21 days due to FcRn binding.
@BioPhys.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: Each record in a NONMEM control stream must begin with a '$' sign. Comments are denoted by a semicolon ';', and NONMEM versions 7.2 and later are case-sensitive.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Basic Structure and Execution of NONMEM

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $PROBLEM record is used to provide a brief description of the model and analysis being performed.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $INPUT record specifies the order of data items in the dataset. Standard names like ID, TIME, DV, AMT, RATE, EVID, MDV, CMT should be used.
The $DATA record specifies the name and location of the dataset file. Options like IGNORE and ACCEPT can be used to filter records.
The $SUBROUTINE record is used to select the appropriate ADVAN (structural model) and TRANS (parameterization) subroutines from the PREDPP library.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Basic Structure and Execution of NONMEM

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $PK block is used to define the pharmacokinetic parameters (e.g., CL, V, KA) and their relationship with covariates.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $ERROR block defines the residual error model, describing the difference between observed and predicted concentrations. Common models include proportional, additive, and combined error models.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $THETA record specifies the initial estimates and boundaries for the fixed-effect parameters (thetas). A common format is (Lower Bound, Initial Estimate, Upper Bound).
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $OMEGA record specifies the initial estimates for the variance of the inter-individual random effects (etas). It is often defined as a diagonal matrix for uncorrelated effects.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $SIGMA record specifies the initial estimates for the variance of the residual error terms (epsilons).
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@ModelingTechniques.rule_description: The $ESTIMATION record specifies the estimation method (e.g., METHOD=FOCE-I) and options like MAXEVAL (maximum evaluations). FOCE-I is generally recommended for PK models.
The $TABLE record specifies which variables to include in the output table file for diagnostics and reporting. Key variables include PRED, RES, WRES, CWRES, and individual parameters.
@ModelingTechniques.source_section: Defining a Model Through the Control Stream

@DataStandards.rule_description: The PC domain is used to store pharmacokinetic concentration data. Key variables include USUBJID, PCTEST, PCORRES, PCSTRESC, PCSTRESN, PCDTC, and VISIT.
The PP domain is used to store pharmacokinetic parameters derived from concentration data. Key variables include USUBJID, PPTEST, PPORRES, PPSTRESC, PPSTRESN, and VISIT.

The ADPC dataset is an analysis-ready dataset for PK concentration data, typically structured as one record per concentration measurement per subject. It should include analysis-specific variables like AVAL, AVISIT, and time-related variables.
@DataStandards.source: Implementation of CDISC ADaM in the Pharmacokinetics Analysis, PHUSE 2014

@DataStandards.rule_description: The ADPP dataset is an analysis-ready dataset for PK parameters, structured as one record per parameter per subject. It is used for statistical analysis of PK parameters and should include variables like PARAM, PARAMCD, and AVAL.
@DataStandards.source: The Creation of ADaM Datasets for Pharmacokinetic (PK) Analysis, Quanticate

@ModelEvaluation.rule_description: Basic goodness-of-fit plots should be generated and inspected, including Observed vs. Predicted concentrations, and residuals (e.g., CWRES) vs. time and predicted concentrations.
@ModelEvaluation.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@ModelEvaluation.rule_description: Conditional Weighted Residuals (CWRES) should be inspected for trends and bias. A general rule of thumb is that the majority of |CWRES| values should be less than 6.
A bootstrap analysis should be performed to assess the stability and uncertainty of the parameter estimates. A high success rate (e.g., >90%) of bootstrap runs is expected.
@ModelEvaluation.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@ModelEvaluation.rule_description: A Visual Predictive Check (VPC) should be performed to evaluate the predictive performance of the model. The observed data's median and prediction intervals should be well-overlaid by the simulated prediction intervals.
@ModelEvaluation.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@ModelEvaluation.rule_description: For nested models, a statistically significant decrease in the Objective Function Value (OFV) (e.g., a drop of 3.84 for one additional parameter, p<0.05) can be used to justify a more complex model.
|IWRES| vs. IPRED should show a horizontal trend line. An upward or downward trend indicates misspecification of the residual error model (e.g., need for proportional vs. additive error).

Eta-shrinkage (IIV shrinkage) should be calculated and reported. High shrinkage (e.g., >30%) may indicate that the data are not informative for estimating the random effect, and covariate relationships for that parameter may be unreliable.
@ModelEvaluation.source: Reporting guidelines for population pharmacokinetic analyses, Dykstra et al., 2015

@CovariateAnalysis.rule_description: A common approach for covariate screening is Stepwise Covariate Modeling (SCM), which involves a forward inclusion step (e.g., p < 0.05, OFV drop > 3.84) and a backward elimination step (e.g., p < 0.01, OFV drop > 6.63).
@CovariateAnalysis.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022

@CovariateAnalysis.rule_description: Continuous covariates (e.g., weight, age) are often modeled using a power function, centered on a typical value from the study population (e.g., (WT/70)^THETA).
@CovariateAnalysis.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@CovariateAnalysis.rule_description: Categorical covariates (e.g., sex, race) are typically modeled as a proportional shift on the parameter for one of the categories.
@CovariateAnalysis.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@CovariateAnalysis.rule_description: A statistically significant covariate effect may not be clinically relevant. The magnitude of the effect should be assessed to determine if it warrants a dose adjustment. An effect of >20-30% on PK parameters is often considered clinically relevant.
@CovariateAnalysis.source: Reporting guidelines for population pharmacokinetic analyses, Dykstra et al., 2015

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: The starting dose for FIH studies should be selected based on preclinical PK/PD data and safety margins. For mAbs, a MABEL (Minimal Anticipated Biological Effect Level) approach is often used.
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: Intensive PK sampling should be implemented for the initial dose cohorts in FIH studies to characterize the full PK profile, especially to detect potential nonlinear PK (TMDD).
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: TMDD should be suspected if dose-normalized exposure (AUC) decreases with decreasing dose, or if clearance is higher at lower doses. This indicates target saturation is occurring.
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: When TMDD is present, a mechanistic TMDD model (e.g., full TMDD, quasi-steady-state (QSS), or Michaelis-Menten approximation) should be used to describe the PK, rather than a simple linear model.
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: Target-mediated Drug Disposition Model for Drugs That Bind More Than One Target, Gibiansky et al., 2010

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: The impact of anti-drug antibodies (ADA) on PK should be evaluated. ADA can increase mAb clearance and reduce exposure. This can be modeled as a time-varying covariate or by analyzing PK in ADA-positive vs. ADA-negative subjects.
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: Tutorial on Monoclonal Antibody Pharmacokinetics and Its Considerations in Early Development, Ovacik & Lin, 2018

@mAb_EarlyClinical.rule_description: An exposure-response (E-R) analysis should be conducted to establish the relationship between drug exposure and efficacy/safety, which informs the selection of an optimal dose and regimen.
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022
@mAb_EarlyClinical.source_section: III.B. Application of Population PK Analysis to Inform Drug Use in Labeling

@Reporting.rule_description: The population PK study report submitted to the FDA should be comprehensive, including sections for executive summary, introduction, methods (data, software, models), results, discussion, and conclusions.
@Reporting.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022
@Reporting.source_section: VII.A. Format and Content of the Population PK Study Report

@Reporting.rule_description: All electronic files, including datasets (in a transport format like .xpt), model code (control streams), output files, and the report, must be submitted to the agency to ensure full traceability and reproducibility.
@Reporting.source: FDA Population Pharmacokinetics Guidance for Industry, 2022
@Reporting.source_section: VII.B. Submitting Electronic Files to the Agency

@Reporting.rule_description: The report abstract or summary should clearly state the objectives, methods, key results (including final model parameters and significant covariates), and conclusions of the analysis.
@Reporting.source: Reporting guidelines for population pharmacokinetic analyses, Dykstra et al., 2015

@Reporting.rule_description: The methods section should be detailed enough to allow an independent modeler to reproduce the analysis. This includes a clear description of the software, model development process, covariate analysis, and validation methods.
@Reporting.source: Reporting guidelines for population pharmacokinetic analyses, Dykstra et al., 2015

@Reporting.rule_description: The results section should present parameter estimates with their uncertainty (e.g., RSE% or 95% CI from bootstrap), goodness-of-fit plots, and validation results (e.g., VPC, bootstrap results).
@Reporting.source: Reporting guidelines for population pharmacokinetic analyses, Dykstra et al., 2015
